speaker: interviewer
text: March the 4th, 1996, Jerry Goose. Interviewer is Sharon Richman. London, Ontario, Canada. The language of the interview is English. Today is March the 4th, 1996. I am interviewing Jerry Goose in the city of London, Ontario, Canada. The language of the interview is English, and my name is Sharon Richman. Would you state your name and your birthday, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Yerucham. No, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just your name and your birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yerucham Goose. That's in English. In Polish, Guz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1919. 20th of December. 1919, the 20th of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berezne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berezne is [? Wolyn ?] on-- the province is Rivne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the nearest large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rivne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big city was Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 3,500, 4,000 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the population of the city altogether, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in the city were Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole city was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the heart of the city was not Jewish. Ukraine is [INAUDIBLE]. There's Ukraine Polish. It was called the Ukraine, got a part. In the city, either Polish, the Jewish people, and then was the army. The Polish army in the city, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But no non-Jewish people in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the nearest city that non-Jewish people lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the city, the villages. Farmers, like, say this. Heart of the city is not farmers. It's not that big city, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So can you describe what the Jewish community was like in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish community-- about religion, or say about some other things?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just whatever you think of.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people-- there was the most business people in the city. About 70% business people, 80%, and 20% workers. And then, the most people, the Jewish people was more religion. Come Friday night, Friday after 4 or 5 o'clock, the whole store is closed. And when you want to pay $10 [INAUDIBLE] something to buy, you couldn't find that. And everybody went to shul. And then Saturday, the same thing as Friday night. And Saturday, the same thing. Close every store. Because the whole city was Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what did your family do? My family used to be butchers. My father's a butcher, and I got in two brothers. Well, I would say one got a butcher shop, and got a store to sell meat, kosher meat. And one brother just bought cattle and passed them to my parents. Until my brother sold them and made profit on them. How many people were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was two, three-- one, two, and father's three, mother's four, and my sister's five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is-- my father is Mottel Guz, Gnesha. And then was Gittel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Gnesha was my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sister was Gittel. Then it was my sister's kids, too, grandchildren, what my mother used to have. There's a lot of them. I couldn't tell you the name correctly of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of your sister's children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either of your brothers' children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my brothers, Not their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Where were you in the family? The oldest, the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what are some of your early memories from being a little boy in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just like usual. In Berezne, the kids, they put them before they're grown up to six, seven years, they get in a cheder, in a rabbi's special room. They used to-- then, later we got a Talmud Torah for grown-ups. And I was grown up, then I went later to Talmud Torah. And then I went to the Polish school after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, do you remember your first day going to the cheder or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a little bit. I remember that I couldn't walk myself. My mother used to walk me over to the cheder. There was one room. But the rabbi-- and he got about 10 kids. And he was teaching the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the kinds of games you'd like to play when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would have got-- I liked-- like they say now-- they say now, it's soccer, I used to like. And tennis. I used to play tennis other way, you know. That's what we used to play, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your best friends from when you were a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember, just I couldn't remember too much the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what can you tell me about these boys you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- I don't remember them. You see, after [? reparation, ?] I don't remember so much like I used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about what some of the holidays were like in your house? The Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish holidays was, come Friday night, which is [INAUDIBLE]. Or Rosh Hashanah night. Friday nights, father used to go in the shul, [INAUDIBLE] the prayer. If the case was a few poor people, what they got no food, no home. He took to meet them, the hungry people, and brought them to the house. Friday night, and they served the dinner. He was between us, serving dinner. And the same Saturday morning. Anybody was there, the same thing. Anybody was hungry, he took them home after dinner time, and they served them dinner. Then, later afternoon, there was [INAUDIBLE]. Maybe you know that. Then used to come the [INAUDIBLE] in the synagogue, and pick up food for the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me why? Tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Passover is the famous holiday because then, they went to the-- they bought some material. They went to the tailor, and they made a suit up. And got a shoemaker and made some shoes up. And it was interesting. Then, when we went to the synagogue, I was looking to the shoes. They were shiny, see if anybody looks at them. I was so excited for the Passover. This the only time what we all dressed up new everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special way of making matzohs in your city for Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We, in our town, there was a-- then, what the Talmud Torah what I tell you, they used to have a basement in this building. And they built up ovens. And three, four people was in the oven, baking the matzohs. And then we used to buy them by the pound. They put in a big box on the shoulders. And two people carry the matzohs to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berezne, on [? Cupernica, ?] the main street. And we got-- we used to have a big house, two stories with a balcony. It was the only house in Berezne, we got this kind of house. In this house, we got about, what, maybe about 12, 14 rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have help in the house? People to help your mother with the housework?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got a steady girl. And some comes and help Ma too. And the laundry was sent out, to wash the laundry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was comfortably off in Berezne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was comfortably off. Because we got the butcher shop. And then we used to supply meat for the army. Every-- we used to take a contract, let's say, for a year. There was an auction. And my father paid the biggest-- the lowest price to deliver the meat. And then he got the auction to give beef to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have any kind of position in the Jewish community? Was he active?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was active in this-- [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] that there was. You know what is a [INAUDIBLE]. In this event for collection for poor people, for supporting people, people you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother used to work in the store, by the meat. She used to sell the meat. My father used to-- help him, too, before he bought the cattle and the farms, he was at market. And then got them to the slaughterhouse. And then in the morning, we brought the meat over to the store. He carved them in pieces. He prepared, and mother used to sell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who looked after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who looked after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to have a sister, Gittel, that I mentioned.

speaker: interviewer
text: She took care of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She took care of me, when I was little, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- your city was mostly all Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware of any anti-Semitism? Was there any that you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There [INAUDIBLE], not in my city. Jews-- in '39, it was known that in Rivne, the big town, they wouldn't let in Jewish kids for university, you know, when they have to go on far for school. It was just public school. In bigger schools, they was against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your particular city, which was almost all Jewish, you weren't aware of any kind of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not what I remember. Because the most anti-Semitism was given by the Polish people. The Ukraine, and the farmers, were the worst. They never know so much about what goes on in the world. They know just in the morning, wake up 5 o'clock, go out in the field, and the cattle. And that's all they know. They don't know nothing. Anything, nothing about what goes on in the world. In fact, we, in the bigger town, don't know either. Because radio is not-- it was not radios, and it was not television. And our paper used to come in once in a month. If you were in Rivne, delivered papers, a newspaper. And that's all we know what goes on in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have electricity in your house or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got electricity. In fact, we once made a collection for the poor people, the [INAUDIBLE] committee. You know what a [INAUDIBLE] committee. A [INAUDIBLE] committee means when some-- no parents, just kids alone. It was a group what they made some [INAUDIBLE]. And sometimes they tried to show some radio. And we used to have a big building, in the rented-- not rented, they give us a big room. And they start showing the radios. And [INAUDIBLE] we couldn't understand not a word. Between time it was common for 20 kids, 30 kids, and listen that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, of course. It was Poland then. I spoke Polish, because I was-- myself was selling meat, too. When we got the army, I used to prepare the meat for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you learned to speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since six year, because everybody's Polish, they're speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you started to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then we'd gotten better. Better Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism. In the Polish school that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not what I remember. Not-- because it was a small school. And I don't think [INAUDIBLE]. Because I was between the smaller kids, six, seven-year-olds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any kinds of Jewish or Zionist organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I think I tell you last time. I forgot already what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hashomer Hatzair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Hashomer Hatzair. In Gordonia. I was there twice. See [INAUDIBLE]. In two place. First, I was in Gordonia. Then there was not enough kids, and I went to Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing in those organizations? What kind of activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they was telling us about Israel, and telling us we have to buy some trees. It make the Germans [INAUDIBLE] singing. And then we'd make some visits from one town to the other town, just like, you know. You make some-- in the evening [? expire ?] in the woods. So fathers in the evening, it was come together four, five cities, together with the [INAUDIBLE]. Then we enjoy it together..

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a boy just growing up, what were your plans? What did you hope to do when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never think what I'm going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you finished school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I think we were 14 year olds.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my bar mitzvah. My bar mitzvah was not very big like in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My bar mitzvah, when I was 13 years, my dad went to the market and bought a small bottle whiskey. And my mother make the cake up overnight. And the morning, we went to the synagogue. And that was the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you started to go to work shortly after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, we went to the work. Comes on Saturday, we don't work. But through the week, we went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you work as?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was helping my-- my parents were butcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of training did you have for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just-- between them, I learned some. Between the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I used to have a few girls. Go out with friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did young children, teenagers, do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to dance in the organization, Hashomer and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys and girls together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boys and girls together, because not was-- Hashomer was not a religion, you know that. They danced together. And some they make speeches. Some-- everybody give a speech, what he thinks and what [INAUDIBLE], what they like to be. You know, that's the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gordonia and Hashomer were both Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any young people who actually went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- not that I remember. I remember [INAUDIBLE] used to go around in my town on the-- in this time. And there was-- to every house, they went and they chopped the wood. And they saved up money to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you heard your first-- when the radio finally came to Berezne? That you had a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: The truth, I tell you, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- in Berezne, did you hear anything about the rise of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just find out later. When the Russian come in to us, then the Russian tell us, this one is Germany, but they going to war together. There was already rumors. But not to radio. We haven't got no radio, no paper. Nobody has.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you hear in 1939 when war was declared?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing we find out is when the Russians were walking to us already. Then we know the war is on. The other way, we don't know that. At least, I don't know that. And I don't think nobody knew. I was young then. I think nobody knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change when the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they changed a lot. You see, when you got a clothing store, they went in, they broke down the locks, and took everything out from the clothing store. And they was very poor. It was nightgowns. It was girls with them. They dressed up in nightgowns for dress. They never say what's a nightgown, what's a dress. That's how poor they was. And the rich ones, very rich, they send them out to Siberia. The normal, you know, they just took out everything what they got. And they start trying to look for them for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to your family and your father's butcher shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's butcher shop, beginning, they let us work. And later, they say, you know what, we let you have the store. You could go on. Just they put in so much taxes, you have to close them down as soon as possible, you couldn't exist. And that's sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember what the talk was in your family and around Berezne, and what the rebbe said about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know too much about it, because with the rebbe, I don't got connections. They got my parents, my grandpa used to go to the rebbe. I was not too much with the rebbe for to see the rebbe. That's why I don't got too much recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- you know, kids have their own [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] with this, with that. We never got what the rabbi said. And I'm sure the rabbi don't know too much either. When he don't have a newspaper, he don't have radio, what anybody would know about it? Do you remember your zaide?

speaker: interviewee
text: My zaide? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neither of your zaides? Your grandfathers were both dead before you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was born later, seven, eight years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, my name is after my zaide.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the Russian stay in Berezne?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about two years. And then the Germans-- until they broke up with Germany. Before they was with Germany together. The war, decided Stalin, he was going to take Poland. And the Germans were going to take the other part. And after-- I think six months later, the Germans start going [INAUDIBLE] everything. And the Russians tell us, come with us. Because the Germans are going to kill you. They're going to kill everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my dad used to say, I am not a politician. I never got business with politics. Why they should kill me? In fact, they've got a nice, big home, and make a nice living. And he was happy to show everybody that he didn't want to run away. Because a lot ran away. They was connected with something. With what?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- maybe with-- I don't know. But he was afraid for some politics, not for the Germans. For other things. He was with the Russians, let's say. When the Russians run away, they went with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about you? What did you want to do at that time? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with me, it's the same thing. I was more with my parents. When the parents said we don't go, we're staying here, then I accept his word. OK. Then, when the Germans came in and start fighting, take over Berezne, then we decided-- we used to have a big house, a two story-- to go over to my brother across the road. He got a small house. Everybody's going to sit with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kieva. There was a Kieva. Then-- OK, go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, soon everybody left to my brother. And maybe two hours later, they were shooting-- the Germans was fighting together. And they put in a bomb right in this house what I was, the whole family. They was killing a few friends of my family. And I was crippled too, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't find me. After, they-- this one's crippled. They took him out. And they couldn't find-- they didn't know where I am. And they start looking for me. And they find that I was under the gravel when the house-- then they looking. They find some-- I used to wear boots, leather boots. And they see the boots, and they pulled me out. And they brought me over. Then about 10 miles from [INAUDIBLE], there was a village. And I was there for three, four days and don't know what goes on with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop right now. OK?
